perturbation.original_text: 9. NO WARRANTY. This NDA shall not obligate either party to disclose any Confidential Information to the other party or enter into any further agreement or business arrangement with the other party. ANY INFORMATION EXCHANGED UNDER THIS NDA IS PROVIDED "AS IS". Discloser makes no representation, warranty or guarantee whatsoever about the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. DEFINITIONS. a. “Confidential Information” means any information regardless of form or medium, whether tangible or intangible, including any copies or fixations made thereof that is disclosed (or to which the Recipient is otherwise provided access) by Discloser that is proprietary or confidential to Discloser or its affiliated companies or to their respective customers, suppliers or other business partners including, without limitation, information that specifies, concerns or is related to Discloser’s intellectual property, trade secrets, business operations, finances, customers, technical know-how, prototypes, designs, processes, products, services, or the development, testing or commercial exploitation of any of the foregoing that is either specifically identified as confidential prior to or at the time of its disclosure or that would reasonably be considered by a person knowledgeable in the industry to be proprietary or confidential in nature because of legends or other markings on the information, the circumstances of disclosure or the nature of the information itself. ANY INFORMATION EXCHANGED UNDER THIS NDA IS PROVIDED "AS IS". Discloser makes no representation, warranty or guarantee whatsoever about the Confidential Information. b. “Discloser” means the party disclosing information. c. “Recipient” means the party receiving the disclosed Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'No Warranty' clause to the 'Definitions' section makes it less visible. Warranty disclaimers should be conspicuous and easily found by the reader.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Sale of Goods Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.1
perturbation.law_citation: Sale of Goods Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.1, s. 53
perturbation.law_explanation: This clause is intended to protect the discloser from liability in case of a defect of confidential information. Ontario's Sale of Goods Act is intended for sales, but it underscores that disclaimers must be clearly emphasized to be enforced, which is violated when relocated to the definitions section.

perturbation.justification: The changed text moves the 'No Warranty' clause to the 'Definitions' section, making it less visible. This undermines the requirement for warranty disclaimers to be conspicuous, as explained. The provided snippets are not directly related to warranty disclaimers or conspicuousness, but the Law Explanation references Ontario's Sale of Goods Act indirectly as an example to underscore that disclaimers must be clearly emphasized to be enforced. Moving the clause to the definitions makes it difficult for a reader to find and creates a structural flaw, which, as described in the Perturbation Definitions, creates a Legal Contradiction. Therefore, the placement affects its enforceability as the clause is not easy to locate or interpret.

perturbation.original_text: 5. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION. At the Discloser’s request, the Recipient shall promptly return any or all Confidential Information received from the Discloser (including, without limitation, any summaries or copies or Confidential Information) or will certify through an officer of the Recipient that all Confidential Information received from the Discloser, and any summaries or copies thereof, have been destroyed. For greater certainty, failure of the Discloser to make such request of Recipient shall not entitle Recipient to make any further use of the Confidential Information or otherwise extend Recipient’s rights set forth herein after expiration or termination of this NDA and Recipient specifically agrees to cease any further use of Discloser’s Confidential Information in such event.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. TERM AND PROTECTION PERIODS. This NDA shall commence upon the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of one (1) year unless sooner terminated in writing by either party. This NDA will automatically renew for subsequent one (1) year periods on the anniversary of the Effective Date, unless otherwise terminated in writing by either party. All obligations contained herein shall continue to bind the parties, their successors and permitted assigns and representatives, until the latter of (a) expiry or termination of this NDA or (b) three years from disclosure to Recipient. At the Discloser’s request, the Recipient shall promptly return any or all Confidential Information received from the Discloser (including, without limitation, any summaries or copies or Confidential Information) or will certify through an officer of the Recipient that all Confidential Information received from the Discloser, and any summaries or copies thereof, have been destroyed. For greater certainty, failure of the Discloser to make such request of Recipient shall not entitle Recipient to make any further use of the Confidential Information or otherwise extend Recipient’s rights set forth herein after expiration or termination of this NDA and Recipient specifically agrees to cease any further use of Discloser’s Confidential Information in such event.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the clause regarding the return of confidential information into the 'Term and Protection Periods' section creates a structural flaw by associating a procedural requirement with a temporal element. This obscures the independent obligation to return the information, regardless of the NDA's term.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Schedule B
perturbation.law_citation: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Schedule B, s. 4
perturbation.law_explanation: By placing the requirement within the term section, the return of confidential information could be misinterpreted as only relevant during the term of the agreement. However, Ontario's Limitations Act dictates limitation periods for bringing claims and actions, including those related to confidential information even after termination of an agreement. Confusing it with the term length is in violation of proper legal structure.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw by placing the return of confidential information within the 'Term and Protection Periods' section, potentially implying that the obligation to return the information is only relevant during the agreement's term. This contradicts the broader legal understanding that obligations related to confidential information can extend beyond the termination of an agreement, as highlighted by the Limitations Act, which dictates limitation periods for bringing claims even after termination. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the 'Limitations Act, 2002' and its various amendments, suggesting the existence of legal timelines and obligations that extend beyond contractual terms. The modified contract obscures the independent obligation to return confidential information, regardless of the NDA's term, creating a legal contradiction.
